summary:
On 17 December 1903, Orville and Wilbur Wright achieved the first sustained, controlled, powered heavier-than-air flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina. Orville piloted the first flight, which lasted 12 seconds and covered 36 meters. The brothers made four flights that day, the longest lasting 59 seconds and covering 260 meters. The aircraft, later called Flyer I, was a biplane with a 12-horsepower engine that the brothers had largely designed and built themselves.

The Wright brothers' success came after years of methodical experimentation. They studied the work of earlier aviation pioneers including Otto Lilienthal and Octave Chanute, conducted their own wind tunnel experiments, and developed the crucial concept of three-axis control — managing pitch, roll, and yaw independently — that gave their design its practical advantage over competitors. Their background as bicycle manufacturers gave them both practical engineering skills and insight into the balance and control challenges of dynamic systems.

The implications of powered flight were transformative for warfare, commerce, communication, and human geography. Within fifteen years, aircraft were playing a decisive role in [[event:110|World War I]]. By the 1930s, commercial aviation was beginning to shrink the effective distances between continents. By the late twentieth century, air travel had become a routine feature of global life, carrying billions of passengers annually.

The Wright brothers' achievement has become one of the iconic moments in the history of technology, representing the triumph of systematic scientific reasoning over the skepticism that had long held heavier-than-air flight to be impossible.
